figures.caption: Figure 2: Backward APJNs for Derf and pre-LN at multiple values of (σ21, σOV ): ViT vs. theory. (a) Derf with α = 1. (b) pre-LN. Teal circles show backward APJN values J B,b measured in ViT on 100 random training samples from CIFAR-100. Black solid lines show the full-theory predictions from the recurrence relations in Eqs. (8)–(12) with (q0, p0) = (0.3, 0.2), and orange dashed lines show the large-b asymptotic predictions: Eq. (18) in (a) and Eq. (16) in (b).

Figure 9: Backward APJN J B,b for ViT with Derf, together with the full-theory prediction and the asymptotic prediction from Eq. (18). (a) α = 0.3; (b) α = 1.9. Teal circles indicate backward APJN values measured in ViT on 100 random training samples from CIFAR-100. Black solid lines show the full-theory predictions obtained from the recurrence relations (8), (9), and (12) with (q0, p0) = (0.3, 0.2), while orange dashed lines show the large-b asymptotic predictions.

Figure 10: Ratio of the cross-positional Jacobian correlation to the APJN, computed from theory for multiple values of (σ21, σOV ). (a) Ratio K B,b/J B,b. (b) Ratio K b,0/J b,0. The cross-positional Jacobian correlation becomes non-negligible for larger values of σOV .

Figure 12: Pre-LN: Histograms of within-sample relative fluctuations of self-dot products (upper row) and cross-positional dot products between activation vectors (lower row) at multiple transformer blocks.
Figure 13: Pre-LN: Sample-to-sample relative fluctuations of mean self-dot products (left) and mean cross-positional dot products between activation vectors (right) across transformer blocks.

Figure 11: Histograms of normalized activation vector norms and normalized dot products between activation vectors at different positions for four CIFAR-100 training samples, measured after random augmentation, feature extraction, and positional encoding, immediately before the first transformer block.
Figure 18: Upper: test performance curves; lower: training curves for the training-stability comparison between Derf and pre-LN when varying the weight standard deviation, shown in Fig. 4(b).
Figure 17: Upper: test performance curves; lower: training curves for the training-stability comparison between Derf and pre-LN when varying depth, shown in Fig. 4(a).

Figure 16: Gradient amplification in the ViT model from (upper row) the last transformer block to earlier blocks, and from (lower row) the final normalization layer to earlier blocks: Derf vs. pre-LN. (a) Depth B = 12. (b) Depth B = 36. As the network depth increases, the gradient amplification in pre-LN matches that of Derf models with smaller α. In addition, smaller α results in smaller overall gradient magnitudes because of gradient propagation through the final normalization layer. (c) Depth B = 12, with the MLP and attention weight standard deviations increased by a factor of 4, leading to substantially stronger gradient amplification in Derf than in pre-LN.

Figure 5: (a) Effect of the number of warmup epochs on the training stability of Derf ViT for different α. (b) Effect of the learning rate on the training stability of Derf for different α. Panels (a) and (b) show test accuracy on CIFAR-100 after 10 training epochs. (c) Test accuracy of Derf models with small α over 90 training epochs.

Figure 24: Effect of initializing Derf with an overly small α. (a) Smaller learning rate and (b) larger learning rate than in the main experiment in Fig. 5(c). (c) Same setup as Fig. 5(c), but with the final LayerNorm not replaced by Derf, to rule out an effect of small α acting directly at the output. (d) No weight decay. All four panels suggest that choosing α too small in Derf can slow convergence.
Figure 23: Comparison of a Derf α sweep and a pre-LN γ sweep at depth 12, showing test accuracy and training loss over 20 training epochs.

Figure 6: The activation covariance components (a) Qb and (b) P b, as well as their ratio (c) P b/Qb. Upper row: theoretical values predicted by the recurrence relations (8) and (9); lower row: values measured in a ViT model for a permutation-symmetric input token configuration with (q0, p0) = (1.0, 0.2).

Figure 22: (a) Effect of the number of warmup epochs and (b) effect of the learning rate on the training stability of Derf for different α, with α frozen during training. Comparison to Fig. 5 suggests that making α trainable accelerates training convergence and improves stability at larger α and higher learning rates.

Figure 21: (a) Effect of the number of warmup epochs and (b) effect of the learning rate on the training stability of Derf for different α, without weight decay. Comparison to Fig. 5 suggests that moderate weight decay, or its absence, does not significantly affect the training-stability boundary.
Figure 15: Derf, α = 1: Sample-to-sample relative fluctuations of mean self-dot products (left) and mean cross-positional dot products between activation vectors (right) across transformer blocks.

Figure 14: Derf, α = 1: Histograms of within-sample relative fluctuations of self-dot products (upper row) and cross-positional dot products between activation vectors (lower row) at multiple transformer blocks.

Figure 8: (a) Forward APJN J b,0: theory vs. ViT on synthetic permutation-symmetric inputs. Circles denote forward APJN values measured in the ViT model on several permutation-symmetric inputs, for both the pre-LN variant and Derf variants with several values of α. Solid lines denote the forward APJN values predicted by the theoretical model in Eqs. (8)–(12). (b) Per-sample MAPE scores for fits to J b,0 on real CIFAR-100 inputs. The setting (σ21, σOV ) = (0.6, 0.3) shows scores for 300 inputs, whereas the other settings show scores for 100 inputs. Each point corresponds to a randomly selected training example from CIFAR-100.

Figure 7: Backward APJN J B,b: ViT on CIFAR-100 inputs versus theory fit. (a) and (b) show two different CIFAR-100 input samples. Circles denote APJN values measured in ViT models (the pre-LN variant and Derf variants with multiple values of α). Solid lines denote the backward APJN values predicted by the theoretical model in Eqs. (8)–(12) using the best-fitting initial condition (q0, p0).

Figure 4: Training stability comparison between Derf and pre-LN ViTs. (a) Test accuracy on CIFAR-100 after 20 epochs of training vs. model depth. (b) Test accuracy on CIFAR-100 after 15 epochs of training versus the MLP and attention weight standard deviation scale (depth B = 12). Panels (a) and (b) should not be compared directly, as they use different batch sizes (128 and 256, respectively).

Figure 19: Training stability comparison between Derf and pre-LN as depth varies, using (a) a lower learning rate and (b) a larger batch size than in the main experiment in Fig. 4(a). All other parameters are kept identical. Both panels show test accuracy on CIFAR-100 after 20 training epochs versus depth. Compared to Fig. 4(a), both decreasing the learning rate and increasing the batch size shift the instability boundary toward larger α and larger depth.

Figure 20: Training stability comparison between Derf and pre-LN as depth varies, using (a) a longer warmup and (b) no weight decay, relative to the main experiment shown in Fig. 4(a). All other parameters are kept identical. Both panels show test accuracy on CIFAR-100 after 20 training epochs as a function of depth. Compared to Fig. 4(a), increasing the number of warmup epochs improves performance near the instability boundary without substantially shifting it, while removing weight decay does not substantially affect the training stability.

Figure 1: (a) Backward APJN J B,b: theory vs. ViT on synthetic permutation-symmetric inputs. Circles denote backward APJN values measured in the ViT model on several permutation-symmetric input token configurations, for both the pre-LN variant and Derf variants with several values of α. Solid lines denote the backward APJN values predicted by the theoretical model in Eqs. (8)–(12). (b) Per-sample MAPE scores for fits to J B,b on CIFAR-100 inputs. The setting (σ21, σOV ) = (0.6, 0.3) shows scores for 300 inputs, whereas the other settings show scores for 100 inputs. Each point corresponds to a randomly selected training example from CIFAR-100, for which we measure the backward APJNs and fit them by choosing the initial conditions (q0, p0).
